title: Building a Document Collaboration App in Laravel (Notion-style)
subtitle: Block-based documents, a nested block tree, real-time collaborative editing, sharing and permissions, and version history — the Laravel architecture behind a Notion-style workspace.

sections.h2: How a block-based editor fits together

blocks.p: A Notion-style app is a tree of typed blocks, edited concurrently, that must merge without losing work. The pressure: a document is not a string but a nested structure of blocks (paragraph, heading, list, table, embed); many people type at once; and every change should sync live and be recoverable.

blocks.diagram:
Workspace
 └─ Page (a document, itself a block)
     └─ Blocks (ordered tree)
         ├─ Heading
         ├─ Paragraph
         ├─ Toggle ─► child blocks (nesting)
         ├─ List item ─► child blocks
         └─ Embed / Table / Image
   + Permissions (page-level share)
   + Versions (snapshots / op log)

blocks.p: The defining action — editing a block — persists a granular change and syncs it:

blocks.diagram:
User edits block ──► PATCH /blocks/{id} {content, op}
   │ authorize (can edit page?)
   ▼
 Apply change to block (content JSON) — granular, not whole-page
   │
   ├─► broadcast BlockUpdated to page.{id}  (others merge live)
   └─► append to version/op log (queued)

ul: Block model — a page is a block; blocks form an ordered, nested tree with typed content.
Concurrent editing — granular block-level changes + a conflict strategy (block-locking, OT, or CRDT).
Real-time sync — broadcasting per-block changes to all viewers.
Sharing & versions — page-level permissions and a recoverable history.

blocks.callout: A block editor is an ordered nested tree of typed blocks edited concurrently. The hard parts are the block tree (ordering + nesting) and merging simultaneous edits without clobbering.
blocks.title: The mental model in one sentence

blocks.p: A block has a type (heading/paragraph/list/table/embed), a JSON content, a parent_id (for nesting), and a position (for ordering among siblings). A page is itself a block. This uniform model lets any block contain any block.

Blocks form a tree via parent_id and order among siblings via position (use fractional/lexorank, as in a Kanban board, so inserting a block is one write). Rendering = recursively fetch children ordered by position. Beware deep recursion; cap nesting or load lazily.

Saving the entire document on each keystroke is catastrophic for collaboration — two users overwrite each other. Instead persist per-block changes: a PATCH touches one block's content. Smaller writes mean smaller conflict windows.

Three options, increasing complexity: (1) Block-level locking / last-write-wins — simplest, occasional lost edits within a block; fine for light collaboration. (2) Operational Transformation (OT) — transform concurrent ops against each other; powers Google Docs, complex. (3) CRDTs (e.g. Yjs) — conflict-free merge by construction; increasingly the default for rich editors, with the server as a relay/persistence layer.

With a CRDT the heavy merge logic lives client-side; Laravel's job is to relay updates over WebSockets and persist the document state/op log so a new client can load and catch up. The server becomes an authority for permissions and storage, not text merging.

Permissions are per page, often inherited down a page tree: a user/group has a role (view/comment/edit/full). Resolve effective access by walking up to the nearest explicit grant. Public share links are tokens with a scope and optional expiry.

Recoverability comes from either periodic snapshots of the block tree or replaying the op log to a point in time. Snapshots are simple to restore; op logs are granular but heavier to replay. Many apps snapshot periodically and keep recent ops.

blocks.callout: Saving the whole document per change is the classic block-editor mistake — it guarantees lost edits under collaboration. Persist granular per-block changes and choose an explicit merge strategy.
blocks.title: Most common misconception

blocks.p: A block editor stores documents as an ordered nested tree of typed blocks, persists granular per-block edits, merges concurrent changes via locking/OT/CRDT, and syncs everything live.

blocks.h4: Analogy — LEGO instructions edited by a team

blocks.p: A page is a model built from LEGO bricks (blocks); each brick has a type and can have bricks stacked on it (nesting). Editing one brick doesn't require rebuilding the whole model (granular edits). When several people build at once, you need a rule for who places a brick in a contested spot — a lock (one at a time), a referee transforming moves (OT), or magic bricks that always click together regardless of order (CRDT). A snapshot is photographing the build so you can rebuild it exactly later.

blocks.diagram:
Page (block)
 ├─ H1   pos 1.0
 ├─ P    pos 2.0
 └─ Toggle pos 3.0
     ├─ P  pos 1.0  (child of toggle)
     └─ List pos 2.0
         └─ Item pos 1.0
 parent_id + position = order within nesting

blocks.p: Each lesson is one idea. Code illustrative.

blocks.h3: Lesson 2 — Insert a block with fractional position
Lesson 3 — Patch one block, not the page
Lesson 7 — Public share links are scoped tokens

items.q: Q1. Why model a page and its contents as one uniform block type?
items.a: A block has type + content + parent_id + position, so any block can contain any block. Treating a page as a block lets nesting, ordering, and rendering use a single recursive model rather than special cases per content type.

items.q: Q2. How is ordering-with-nesting represented in one table?
items.a: parent_id gives the tree; position (fractional/lexorank) orders siblings. Rendering recursively fetches children ordered by position; inserting a block is a single write at the midpoint.

items.q: Q3. Why persist per-block edits instead of saving the whole document?
items.a: Whole-document saves overwrite concurrent edits, guaranteeing lost work. Per-block PATCHes shrink the write and the conflict window, enabling real collaboration.

items.q: Q4. Compare last-write-wins, OT, and CRDT.
items.a: LWW/lock: simplest, lossy on conflict, fine for light use. OT: transforms concurrent ops, strong but complex (Google Docs). CRDT: conflict-free merge by construction, medium-high complexity, modern default with the server as relay/persistence.

items.q: Q5. With a CRDT, what is Laravel's role?
items.a: Relay opaque updates over WebSockets and persist the state/op log so late joiners can catch up. The merge logic is client-side; the server owns permissions and storage, not text merging.

items.q: Q6. How are effective page permissions resolved when grants are inherited?
items.a: Walk up the page tree from the page to the root, returning the role from the nearest explicit grant for the user/group; no grant means no access.

items.q: Q7. Two ways to implement version history, and their trade-off?
items.a: Periodic snapshots of the block tree (simple to restore, coarser) vs replaying an append-only op log (granular, heavier to reconstruct). Many apps combine periodic snapshots with recent ops.

blocks.h3: Code drill — concurrent block edits don't clobber the page (Pest)

blocks.p: Re-do the quiz from memory; redraw the block tree each session.

head: When
Session (~10–15 min)
Goal
rows: Read packet; quiz with answers open.
Flashcards; explain LWW vs OT vs CRDT aloud.
Interleaved quiz; write the per-block PATCH from blank.
Teach the concurrency spectrum to someone.
Closed-book interleaved pass; misses restart Day 1.

blocks.callout: Retrieving just before you'd forget produces the strongest gain.
blocks.title: Why the gaps grow

blocks.p: Topics shuffled to train choosing the right idea.

items.q: M1. Two users typing in the same paragraph keep overwriting each other. Diagnosis and options?
items.a: Whole-block last-write-wins with no merge. Options in order of effort: block-level locking, character-level OT, or a CRDT (Yjs) where the server relays/persists opaque updates and clients merge.

items.q: M2. Rendering a deeply nested page is slow / stack-overflows. Two fixes?
items.a: (1) Cap nesting depth or load child blocks lazily on expand. (2) Fetch the whole tree in one query and build it in memory (avoid per-node N+1 recursion); index (page_id, parent_id, position).

items.q: M3. Inserting a block at the top renumbers all siblings. Better approach?
items.a: Use fractional/lexorank positions: new position = midpoint of neighbours, a single write. Same technique as Kanban card ordering.

items.q: M4. A shared-link recipient can edit though the link was 'view only'. Flaw?
items.a: Share token scope not enforced server-side. The token must map to a role (view) and the policy must check it on every mutation; never trust the client to honour read-only.

items.q: M5. A user moved to a sub-page they shouldn't access. How should permission have resolved?
items.a: By walking up the page tree to the nearest explicit grant; if none grants the user access, deny. Inherited permissions must be resolved server-side per request.

items.q: M6. When is a CRDT overkill?
items.a: For light, mostly single-editor documents or an MVP. Block-level locking or last-write-wins is far simpler and adequate; adopt CRDT/OT when true simultaneous co-editing of the same text is a core requirement.

ul: Build the editor twice — last-write-wins vs a CRDT (Yjs) relayed by Laravel — and feel where each breaks.
Break collaboration on purpose — switch to whole-page saves and watch two editors clobber each other.
Write permission tests first: inherited grants, share-link scope, denied sub-pages.
Add comments & mentions anchored to specific blocks.
Stretch goals: block-level version diffing, offline editing with later sync, slash-command block insertion, and full-text search across pages (Scout).

flashcards: The two hard parts of a block editor
The ordered nested block tree, and merging concurrent edits without clobbering.
Everything-is-a-block model
Block = type + content(JSON) + parent_id + position; a page is itself a block.
Ordering + nesting representation
parent_id for the tree, fractional/lexorank position for sibling order.
Why per-block edits not whole-page saves
Whole-page saves overwrite concurrent edits; granular PATCHes shrink the conflict window.
Concurrency spectrum
Last-write-wins/lock → OT → CRDT, increasing merge quality and complexity.
Laravel's role with a CRDT
Relay opaque updates over WebSockets and persist state/op log for late joiners; merge is client-side.
Resolving inherited permissions
Walk up the page tree to the nearest explicit grant; none = no access.
Two version-history strategies
Periodic tree snapshots (simple restore) or op-log replay (granular, heavier).
Public share link
A scoped token (role + expiry) granting access to a page subtree, enforced server-side.
Inserting a block cheaply
Fractional position = midpoint of neighbours; one write, no sibling renumber.
When CRDT is overkill
Light/single-editor docs or MVPs — locking or LWW is simpler and enough.
Rendering a page
Recursively fetch children ordered by position; load whole tree in one query to avoid N+1.
glossary: A typed unit of content with JSON content, a parent, and a position.
A document, itself modelled as a block.
The nested structure of blocks via parent_id.
A change persisted at block level, not whole-document.
Simplest merge: latest save overwrites; lossy on conflict.
Transforming concurrent ops to merge edits (OT).
Conflict-free Replicated Data Type; merges by construction.
A saved copy of the block tree at a point in time.
An explicit permission (role) on a page for a user/group.
A tokenised, scoped public access URL.
